title: Tax-Efficient Asset Management: Evidence from Equity Mutual Funds
abstract: We investigate the relation between tax burdens and mutual fund performance from both a theoretical and an empirical perspective. The theoretical model introduces heterogeneous tax clienteles in an environment with decreasing returns to scale and shows that the equilibrium performance of mutual funds depends on the size of the tax clienteles. Our empirical results show that the performance of U.S. equity mutual funds is related to their tax burdens. We find that tax-efficient funds exhibit not only superior after-tax performance, but also superior before-tax performance due to lower trading costs, favorable style exposures, and better selectivity.

article.introduction:
Investment taxes have a substantialimpact on the long-term performance of taxable mutual fund investors. Mutual funds in the United States are required to distribute their dividend income and their realized capital gains to their shareholders. These distributions are taxed at the fund investor level at different rates. In particular, short-term capital gains distributions, generated by the liquidation of fund positions held for less than one year, are typically taxed at substantially higher rates than long-term capital gains distributions. Unrealized capital gains, meanwhile, remain untaxed until the securities are liquidated. In this paper, we shed light on the costs and benefits of tax-efficient asset management by investigating the before- and after-tax performance of U.S. equity mutual funds with different tax management strategies.
Mutual funds can reduce the tax burdens of their shareholders by deferring the realization of capital gains, by accelerating the realization of capital losses, and by avoiding securities with high dividend yields, as discussed by Bergstresser and Poterba (2002). Such tax management strategies, however, constrain funds' investment choices and may reduce their before-tax performance. On the other hand, fund managers who are sufficiently savvy to avoid taxes may exhibit superior investment abilities in other dimensions as well. For instance, tax-efficient fund managers may generate lower trading costs, take on favorable factor exposures, and demonstrate superior stock selection and timing abilities.
To better understand the equilibrium relation between before- and after-tax returns across mutual funds, we develop a stylized model with different investment strategies and heterogeneous tax clienteles. Investors have access to investment opportunities that face different tax burdens and that are subject to decreasing returns to scale, as suggested by Berk and Green (2004). The model shows that the returns of the different investment strategies depend on the distribution of tax clienteles. If the proportion of tax-exempt investors is relatively high, then tax-exempt investors are the dominant investors and before-tax returns are equalized across the strategies. Conversely, if taxable investors are dominant, then after-tax returns are equalized. At intermediate distribution levels, neither before-tax nor after-tax returns are equalized, in contrast to the predictions by Berk and Green (2004).
To empirically estimate the relation between tax efficiency and fund performance, we use data on a comprehensive sample of U.S. equity mutual funds between 1990 and 2016. We find that taxes have a significant effect on the performance of taxable fund investors. On average, fund shareholders in the highest tax bracket are estimated to pay investment taxes that amount to 1.08% of their investment value per year. This tax burden is similar in size to fund expenses, which have received substantial attention in the literature (e.g., French (2008)).
We find that the tax burden of mutual funds can be predicted by their investment styles, by the flows of fund investors, and by the capital gains overhang. Tax burdens tend to be higher for funds that focus on small-capitalization and value portfolios, as these investment styles trigger relatively high realizations of capital gains. In addition, tax burdens increase as funds experience redemptions by fund investors or volatile investor flows. Finally, funds' tax burdens are positively related to turnover, fund age, and the capital gains overhang.
We further find that the tax efficiency of mutual funds is highly persistent, as mutual funds tend to maintain consistent styles and investment strategies over time. A one-standard-deviation increase in prior tax burden is associated with a significant increase in the tax burden of a fund over the next year of between 0.44 and 0.47 percentage points.
Turning to our main analysis, we find that the before- and after-tax performance of U.S. equity mutual funds is related to their prior tax burdens. We find that tax-efficient funds generate not only superior after-tax performance, but also superior before-tax performance over the next year. A one-standard-deviation decrease in the tax burden over the prior three years (which is equal to 1.18 percentage points) is associated with a significant increase in thebefore-taxreturn of a fund of between 0.39 and 0.55 percentage points over the next year.
Tax-efficient funds generate superiorafter-taxreturns in the future due both to higher before-tax returns and lower tax burdens. Thus, our results indicate that tax-efficient asset management strategies, as practiced by U.S. equity mutual funds between 1990 and 2016, exhibit favorable returns after as well as before taxes. The superior pre-tax performance of tax-efficient funds is not eliminated by fund flows, as would be expected from the stylized benchmark model with decreasing returns to scale. This puzzling result indicates that fund investors are not fully aware of the benefits of tax efficiency.
To further investigate the pre-tax performance differences across funds with differential tax efficiency, we study the trading costs, style exposures, and investment abilities of mutual funds. The superior pre-tax performance of tax-efficient funds can be explained by lower trading costs, favorable style exposures, and superior investment ability. These results indicate that the investment ability of fund managers is correlated across different dimensions: fund managers who avoided taxable distributions in prior years face lower trading costs, exhibit superior selectivity, and take on favorable style exposures in future years.
Mutual funds might be tax efficient not because these funds deliberately minimize their tax burden, but rather because these funds' strategies generate relatively low tax burdens as a byproduct. For example, a low-turnover fund that invests in large capitalization stocks should be relatively tax efficient because this fund liquidates stock positions infrequently and any such liquidations are concentrated among poorly performing stocks that are excluded from large-capitalization benchmarks. To address this possibility, we study the performance of mutual funds that explicitly classify themselves as tax-managed funds. We find that after taxes, self-designated tax-efficient funds outperform matched funds in the same families with similar investment styles. Furthermore, the self-designated tax-efficient funds do not significantly underperform other funds before taxes, indicating that the constraints imposed by tax-efficient asset management do not appear to have negative performance consequences. Since these matched funds exhibit similar investment styles and have similar management teams, these matching results are consistent with our main results, which indicate that performance differences can be explained by trading costs, style exposures, and investment abilities.
Our paper is related to a small literature that investigates the tax implications of mutual fund management. Jeffrey and Arnott (1993), Dickson and Shoven (1995), and Arnott, Kalesnik, and Schuesler (2018) show that investment taxes play an important role for mutual fund investors. Barclay, Pearson, and Weisbach (1998) discuss the conflict that mutual fund managers face in designing their capital gains distribution policy, arguing that managers have an incentive to realize some capital gains, and thereby reduce the capital gains overhang, to attract prospective investors. Bergstresser and Poterba (2002) explore the relation between the after-tax returns that taxable investors earn on equity mutual funds and subsequent cash inflows to these funds. They also analyze the determinants of mutual funds' tax burdens. Gibson, Safieddine, and Titman (2000) find evidence of mutual fund managers engaging in tax loss selling at the end of the tax year. Christoffersen et al. (2006) find that managers' decisions with respect to cross-border dividend payments differ according to the proportion of defined contribution (DC) assets in their funds. Sialm and Starks (2012) focus on mutual fund clienteles and study whether the investment strategies and performance of mutual funds differ between funds held primarily by DC pension investors and funds held by other investors. In contrast, we study the relation between the tax efficiency of mutual funds and their subsequent performance before and after taxes. Although tax clienteles and tax burdens are related, the two measures capture different characteristics of mutual funds.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. SectionIpresents a stylized model of delegated portfolio management with heterogeneous tax clienteles. SectionIIsummarizes our data sources, defines the tax burden, and reports summary statistics. SectionIIIestimates the key determinants of mutual funds' tax burdens. SectionIVinvestigates whether the tax efficiency of a mutual funds is related to the before- and after-tax performance of U.S. equity mutual funds. SectionVstudies the subsample of mutual funds that self-designate as tax managed. Finally, SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Model:
In this section, we present a stylized model in which heterogeneous tax clienteles have access to investment opportunities that face differential taxation and that are subject to decreasing returns to scale, as proposed by Berk and Green (2004). The model allows us to examine whether before- or after-tax returns are equalized in equilibrium.1
The model includes two types of investors with differential tax treatmenti∈{X,T}. Each investor type consists of a large number of individual investors who take prices as given and maximize their utility by selecting an optimal portfolio. Returns of tax-exempt investors, who we denote byX, are not taxed, whereas returns of taxable investors, who we denote byT, are taxed at a flat rate ofτs, which depends on the investment strategy. Tax-exempt assets are held by tax-exempt institutions or charities or in tax-qualified retirement accounts (e.g., IRAs, 401(k), 403(b)). Taxable assets are held in regular taxable accounts.
Investment taxes are symmetric between capital gains and capital losses and do not explicitly allow for tax deferral. StrategyHexhibits a higher tax burden than strategyL, either because it invests in assets that are taxed at higher rates or because it has a higher propensity to realize capital gains. Thus,0<τL<τH.
The proportion of wealth held by taxable investors amounts toλ∈[0,1]. The remaining assets (i.e.,1−λ) are held by tax-exempt investors.
Both types of investors are risk-neutral and cannot hold short positions or incur debt (wsi≥0). Short-selling constraints are necessary with risk-neutral agents, as otherwise agents will take infinitely large short and long positions in the two investment strategies to take advantage of the differential tax treatment. Investors avoid such tax arbitrage strategies at moderate levels of risk aversion.2
Panel A of Figure1depicts the before-tax returns of the two investment strategies in an environment with only tax-exempt investors.3The horizontal axis shows the allocation of assets across the two investment options. The amount invested in the low-tax assetwLis measured from the left to the right, whereas the amount invested in the high-tax assetwH=1−wLis measured from the right to the left.
The declining curve labeledrLshows that the return of the low-tax strategy decreases as the assets invested in this strategy increase. The highest return,rL=fL(0)(i.e., intercept on the left vertical axis), occurs if no funds are invested in this strategy, while the lowest return,rL=fL(1)(i.e., intercept on the right vertical axis), occurs if all funds are invested in this strategy. The increasing curve labeledrHshows that the return of the high-tax strategy increases as the assets invested in this strategy decrease. The intercept on the left vertical axis shows the return of the high-tax strategy if all funds are invested in this strategy (i.e.,fH(1)) and the intercept on the right vertical axis shows the return of the high-tax strategy if no funds are invested in this strategy (i.e.,fH(0)).4
An optimal portfolio allocation for tax-exempt investors occurs when the returns of the two investment strategies are equalized, which is depicted in Panel A as the intersection of the two curves.5The returns of the two investment strategies will be equal (i.e.,rL=fL(λ̲)=fH(1−λ̲)=rH) at the allocationwL=λ̲andwH=1−λ̲. Other allocations are suboptimal as they generate lower portfolio returns.
Taxable investors allocate their portfolios according to the strategies' after-tax returns. The after-tax returns are given by(1−τH)rHfor the high-tax strategy and(1−τL)rLfor the low-tax strategy. Panel B of Figure1depicts the after-tax returns of the two investment strategies using dashed curves.
As can be seen in Panel B, an optimal portfolio allocation for taxable investors occurs when the after-tax returns of the two investment strategies are equalized.6The after-tax returns of the two investment strategies will be equal (i.e.,(1−τL)rL=(1−τL)fL(λ¯)=(1−τH)fH(1−λ¯)=(1−τH)rH) at the allocation ofwL=λ¯andwH=1−λ¯. Other allocations are suboptimal as they generate lower expected portfolio returns after taxes.
In the first case, the proportion of taxable investors is low (i.e.,λ≤λ̲). In this case, tax-exempt investors are the dominant investors and they hold both investment strategies in equilibrium. To make them indifferent between investing in the two strategies, the before-tax returns of the two strategies need to be equal (i.e.,rH=rL). The taxable investors will invest all of their wealth in the low-tax strategy since it offers a higher after-tax return. Thus, the taxable investors will put all of their wealth in the low-tax strategy,wLT=1, and nothing in the high-tax strategy,wHT=0. The tax-exempt investors will allocate their wealth to equilibrate the before-tax returns since they are the dominant investors. Their allocation to the low-tax strategy is equal towLX=(λ̲−λ)/(1−λ)≥0. The total amount invested in the low-tax strategy iswL=λwLT+(1−λ)wLX=λ×1+(1−λ)(λ̲−λ)/(1−λ)=λ̲. At these allocations, the before-tax returns of the two strategies are equalized (rL=fL(λ̲)=fH(1−λ̲)=rH) and the after-tax return of the low-tax strategy exceeds the after-tax return of the high-tax strategy ((1−τL)rL=(1−τL)fL(λ̲)>(1−τH)fH(1−λ̲)=(1−τH)rH). Thus, neither taxable nor tax-exempt investors have an incentive to deviate from the allocations given above.
Figure2depicts the equilibrium allocations and returns. Panel A corresponds to the portfolio allocations of the two clienteles. The horizontal axis shows the proportion of taxable investors λ. The vertical axis shows the portfolio weights invested in the low-tax asset by taxable investors (wLT), by tax-exempt investors (wLX), and in the aggregate (wL=λwLT+(1−λ)wLX). The weights allocated to the high-tax asset are given by1−wLi. When the proportion of taxable investors is low (i.e.,λ≤λ̲), taxable investors will allocate all of their wealth in the low-tax strategy (i.e.,wLT=1), whereas tax-exempt investors will hold both strategies and investwLX=(λ̲−λ)/(1−λ)≥0in the low-tax strategy. In this case, the aggregate weight in the low-tax asset is constant and equal towL=λwLT+(1−λ)wLX=λ̲.
In the second case, the proportion of taxable investors is intermediate (i.e.,λ̲<λ<λ¯). In this case, it is optimal for investors to fully concentrate in one investment strategy: taxable investors will allocate all of their wealth to the low-tax strategy (i.e.,wHT=0andwLT=1), whereas tax-exempt investors will allocate all of their wealth to the high-tax strategy (i.e.,wHX=1andwLX=0). Tax-exempt investors do not have an incentive to deviate from this strategy since the before-tax return of the high-tax strategy is higher than the before-tax return of the low-tax strategy (i.e.,rH=fH(1−λ)>fL(λ)=rL). Similarly, taxable investors do not have an incentive to deviate since the after-tax return of the low-tax strategy is higher than the after-tax return of the high-tax strategy (i.e.,(1−τL)rL=(1−τL)fL(λ)>(1−τH)fH(1−λ)=(1−τH)rH), as can be seen in Panel B of Figure1.
In the third case, the proportion of taxable investors is relatively high (i.e.,λ≥λ¯). In this case, taxable investors are the dominant investors and they hold both investment strategies in equilibrium. To make them indifferent between investing in the two strategies, the after-tax returns of the two strategies need to be equal (i.e.,(1−τH)rH=(1−τL)rL). The minority of tax-exempt investors will find it optimal to invest all of their wealth in the high-tax strategy (i.e.,wHX=1) since it offers a higher before-tax return than the low-tax strategy. The taxable investors will allocate their wealth to equilibrate the after-tax returns. Their allocation to the low-tax strategy is equal towLT=λ¯/λ≤1. The total amount invested in the low-tax strategy iswL=λwLT+(1−λ)wLX=λ×λ¯/λ+(1−λ)×0=λ¯. At these allocations, the after-tax returns of the two strategies are equalized ((1−τL)rL=(1−τL)fL(λ¯)=(1−τH)fH(1−λ¯)=(1−τH)rH) and the before-tax returns of the high-tax strategy exceed the before-tax returns of the low-tax strategy (rH=fH(1−λ¯)>fL(λ¯)=rL). Thus, neither taxable nor tax-exempt investors have an incentive to deviate from the allocations given above. With a relatively high proportion of taxable investors, the after-tax returns are equalized across the two strategies, but the before-tax returns differ across the two strategies, as depicted in Figure2.
The stylized model makes two main predictions. First, investors tend to tilt their portfolios toward the investment strategy that corresponds to their tax preferences—tax-exempt investors tend to tilt their portfolios toward the investment strategy that is more heavily taxed compared to taxable investors. The extent of the tilting depends on the distribution of investor types. A perfect separation into two tax clienteles occurs if clienteles are fairly equally distributed. Second, the returns of the different strategies depend on the distribution of tax clienteles. If the proportion of tax-exempt investors is relatively high, then tax-exempt investors are the dominant investors and before-tax returns are equalized across the strategies. If taxable investors are dominant, after-tax returns are equalized. At intermediate distribution levels, neither before- nor after-tax returns are equalized, in contrast to the predictions of the model by Berk and Green (2004). In the stylized model, the equilibrium before-tax returns on the high-tax strategy are at least as high as the before-tax returns on the low-tax strategy, while the after-tax returns on the low-tax strategy are at least as high as the after-tax returns on the high-tax strategy. In the remainder of the paper, we study empirically whether before- or after-tax returns are equalized using a sample of domestic equity mutual funds.
article.II. Data and Summary Statistics:
In this section, we summarize our data sources, define the tax burden of equity mutual funds, and report summary statistics for our main variables.
Our data cover U.S. equity mutual funds over the period 1990 to 2016 based on the CRSP Survivorship Bias Free Mutual Fund database.8The CRSP database includes mutual fund characteristics such as fund returns, fund dividend and capital gains distributions, total net assets (TNA), fees, flows, and investment objectives. We exclude balanced, bond, international, and money market funds, as well as funds that on average hold less than 80% of their assets in common stock if the investment objective is missing. To avoid the incubation bias identified by Evans (2010), we also exclude funds that in the previous month manage less than $10 million, funds with missing fund names in the CRSP database, and funds for which the observation year is in the same year or in an earlier year than the reported fund start. Mutual fund share classes are aggregated at the fund level. We aggregate returns, fund distributions, expense ratios, and turnover ratios by asset-weighting the corresponding characteristics of the individual share classes. The TNA of a fund is aggregated by summing the TNAs of the individual share classes, and fund age is set to the age of the oldest share class. Our sample includes 48,567 annual fund observations.
We merge the CRSP mutual fund database with the Thomson-Reuters Mutual Fund Holdings database and the CRSP Stock Database using the MFLINKS file based on Wermers (2000) and available through Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS). The Thomson-Reuters data include mutual funds' equity holdings on specific disclosure dates, which allow us to determine the style of the stocks being held and the capital gains overhangs.
We obtain data on the top marginal federal tax rates on dividends, short-term capital gains, and long-term capital gains from the Statistics of Income database of the Internal Revenue Service (IRS).9
Finally, we obtain the proportion of DC assets in mutual funds between 1997 and 2016 from surveys conducted byPensions & Investments. In these surveys, mutual fund management companies are asked to report the dollar amount of mutual fund assets held in DC retirement accounts for the mutual funds most used by DC plans in broad investment categories (Domestic Equity Funds, Domestic Fixed Income Funds, International Equity Funds, Balanced Funds, Money Market Funds).10
Although mutual funds are considered corporations in the United States, their income is usually not double taxed because mutual funds registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 can pass their dividend and capital gains income through to fund shareholders. Thus, an investment company distributing its realized income to its shareholders has no direct tax liability. However, these distributions are taxable to mutual fund shareholders who hold the mutual fund in a taxable account. Thus, when funds realize capital gains, they accelerate the payment of taxes for their current shareholders.
We obtain mutual funds' dividend and capital gains distributions from the CRSP mutual fund database. Short-term gains typically correspond to investments held for less than a year and long-term gains typically correspond to investments held for one year or more. Taxes are not taken into account for nontaxed returns of capital.12Dividend taxes are not charged for untaxed or tax-exempt dividends. Mutual funds are only required to distribute dividends that exceed their fund expenses. Thus, dividend distributions of mutual funds tend to be significantly smaller than the dividends paid by the corporations they hold.13A substantial fraction of dividend and capital-gains distributions occur in December. It is therefore important to aggregate tax burdens and fund distributions over an annual time period.
Figure3summarizes the time-series variation in the average dividend and capital gains distributions by domestic equity mutual funds. Dividend distributions have gradually declined as companies have increasingly replaced dividends with share repurchases, as discussed by Allen and Michaely (2003). Capital gains distributions are cyclical—they are substantial in the 1990s and mid-2000s and are small in the early and late 2000s.
We use the top federal marginal tax rates on dividends and short- and long-term capital gains to compute the tax burden. Figure4summarizes the time-series variation in the top federal dividend and capital gains tax rates. Over our sample period there were three major tax reforms. The Taxpayer Relief Act of 1997 under Bill Clinton reduced the top federal long-term capital gains tax rate from 28% to 20% and the Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003 under George W. Bush reduced the marginal federal tax rate on qualified dividends and long-term capital gains to 15%. The tax reforms of 2013 under Barak Obama increased the top marginal income tax rate to 39.6% and increased the top marginal tax rates on qualified dividends and long-term capital gains to 20%. In addition, a 3.8% surtax on investment income was introduced for high-income households.
Figure5depicts the cross-sectional distribution of tax burdens over our sample period based on funds' dividend and capital gains distributions and on the average marginal tax rates. We observe substantial cross-sectional variation in fund distributions and tax burdens. For example, in 2000, funds in the first quartile exhibit annual tax burdens below 0.55% while funds in the fourth quartile exhibit annual tax burdens above 3.55%.
TableIreports the means of the main variables.14The mean tax burden over our sample equals 1.08%. The average tax burden is similar in magnitude to the average expense ratio, which has obtained a lot of attention in the mutual fund literature (Carhart (1997), Wermers (2000), French (2008), Gil-Bazo and Ruiz-Verdu (2009), and Berk and van Binsbergen (2015)).15
Around two-thirds of fund distributions are characterized as long-term capital gains, whereas dividend and short-term capital gains account for 18% and 16% of fund distributions. Short-term capital gains distributions concentrate on a few funds during a few time periods. Funds in our sample have an average expense ratio of 1.14% and exhibit a turnover ratio of 83%. The average fund manages $1,553 million and is around 13 years old.
Since estimated fund flows exhibit substantial outliers, we winsorize both the top and the bottom parts of the distribution at the 2.5% level. Funds in our sample have an average flow of 0.72% per year.
The style scores and the unrealized capital gains overhangs are obtained from Thomson-Reuters mutual fund holdings data. Following Daniel et al. (1997), we group each stock listed in CRSP into respective quintiles according to its market value (using NYSE cutoff levels), its industry-adjusted book-to-market ratio, and its prior-year return. Using the quintile information of stocks held by a mutual fund, we compute the value-weighted size, value, and momentum scores for each fund in each period. For example, a mutual fund that invests one-half of its value in stocks in the largest size quintile and the other half in stocks in the second-largest size quintile has a size score of 4.5. Mutual funds in our sample tend to hold stocks in the largest size quintile and have a slight tilt toward growth and momentum stocks.
Using equity holdings data from Thomson-Reuters, we calculate a mutual fund's short- and long-term capital gains overhang following Sialm and Starks (2012). The capital gains overhang reflects investors' future potential tax burden. Specifically, at the end of each quarter we compute each equity position's unrealized capital gain as the percentage difference between the current price of the position and the price of the position on the last trading day in the quarter the position was acquired. If the current position was acquired across multiple quarters, we compute the weighted-average capital gain of the different lots. An unrealized capital gain is classified as short term if the position has been held for less than four quarters. The unrealized short- and long-term capital gains are then aggregated across all stock positions of a fund. We find that the average short- and long-term capital gain overhangs equal 1.61% and 11.39%, respectively.16
Finally, the average proportion of DC assets based on the surveys byPensions & Investmentsequals 27.26%. The DC ratio is winsorized at the 1% level.
The tax burdens differ significantly by the type of mutual fund, as also illustrated in TableI. Accordingly, we divide our sample into actively managed, tax-managed, and passively managed funds. Tax-managed and passive funds are identified by their names. These fund types account for 1.3% and 6.7% of the observations in our sample, respectively. TableIindicates that tax-managed and passively managed funds tend to exhibit lower tax burdens than actively managed funds.
article.III. Determinants of Tax Burden:
The tax burden of a mutual fund depends on the investment style of the fund, the trading behavior of the fund's investors, and market conditions. The role of a fund's investment style is examined by Bergstresser and Pontiff (2013) and Israel and Moskowitz (2011), who simulate the long-term tax burdens of various investment styles. Funds that tend to hold assets for shorter time periods and exhibit higher portfolio turnover typically incur higher tax burdens because short-term gains are more heavily taxed than long-term gains. Funds that tend to focus on small-capitalization stocks need to liquidate stocks that appreciate in value and exit the small-capitalization benchmarks. Similarly, value funds and contrarian funds need to liquidate stocks that exhibited high recent performance as the underlying stocks become growth or momentum stocks. In addition, large-capitalization stocks and value stocks tend to have higher dividend yields, which increase the tax burden, especially during tax regimes without preferential tax treatment of dividends.17
The tax burden also depends on the trading behavior of fund investors, as discussed by Dickson, Shoven, and Sialm (2000). On the one hand, redemptions of fund investors may force the mutual fund to sell some of its equity positions to pay off the liquidating investors. As a result, the fund may be forced to distribute taxable capital gains to its shareholders. On the other hand, new investors convey a positive externality upon existing investors by diluting the unrealized capital gain position of the fund. Thus, taxes correspond to a source of strategic complementarity across investors in open-ended funds in addition to the liquidity-based externalities discussed by Edelen (1999) and Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang (2010).
Finally, the tax burden depends on market conditions. Capital gains distributions tend to be larger after bull markets and for funds that accumulated a more substantial unrealized capital gains overhang.
TableIIindicates that a fund's investment style plays an important role in determining the tax burden. In particular, funds that hold companies with smaller market capitalizations and with higher book-to-market ratios tend to exhibit higher capital gains distributions and tax burdens. For example, a one-standard-deviation decrease in the size score (0.95 units) increases the tax burden by between 0.09 and 0.10 percentage points, whereas a one-standard-deviation increase in the value score (0.41 units) increases the tax burden by between 0.08 and 0.10 percentage points. Changes in the momentum scores have an economically smaller and statistically insignificant impact on the tax burden.
We further find that funds that experience negative or volatile new money growth over the prior year tend to distribute higher capital gains over the subsequent year since these funds are more likely to sell shares and recognize capital gains. The effect of investor behavior is also economically significant: a one-standard-deviation decline in fund flow (3.15%) and a one-standard-deviation increase in fund flow volatility (2.56%) increase the tax burden by 0.28 and 0.04 percentage points, respectively.
The tax burden increases with fund turnover, as increased turnover raises short-term capital gains distributions. The tax burden decreases with the expense ratio, primarily because fund expenses can be subtracted from funds' distributions.
Finally, the tax burden increases with the short-term capital gains overhang but is not significantly related to the long-term capital gains overhang: a one-standard-deviation increase in the short-term capital gains overhang (5.39%) increases next year's tax burden by 22 basis points. A large capital gains overhang may indicate that the fund will make large distributions in the near future, as it will be forced to realize these gains (Barclay, Pearson, and Weisbach (1998)). On the other hand, funds with large embedded capital gains may continue deferring the realization of capital gains into the future and hence exhibit smaller distributions in future years.
article.IV. Mutual Fund Performance:
In this section, we analyze the main question of whether the before- and after-tax performance of equity mutual funds depend on the funds' prior tax efficiency. Addressing this question sheds light on the economic costs of investment constraints imposed by tax-efficient asset management. Tax-efficient asset management could be sufficiently costly that it reduces before-tax performance by more than the decline in the tax burden. Funds with a low prior tax burden may underperform funds with a high tax burden before or even after taxes. Furthermore, taxes could be capitalized into equity prices (Sialm (2009)), as assets that face a lower tax burden may offer lower before-tax returns. Alternatively, tax-efficient asset management may improve before-tax returns if tax-efficient investment strategies exhibit lower trading costs (e.g., due to lower portfolio turnover or overall awareness of efficient trading strategies) or higher investment returns (e.g., due to superior investment ability or favorable style exposures). In addition, analyzing before- and after-tax returns of different tax strategies can shed light on equilibrium fund returns with heterogeneous clienteles.
The base-case regression includes year fixed effects and style fixed effects, and standard errors are clustered by fund. The nine different fund equity styles are based on fund holdings and are obtained by dividing funds in each period sequentially into three equal-sized groups by their size score (i.e., the market capitalization of their holdings) and into three equal-sized groups by their value score (i.e., the book-to-market ratio of their holdings).
The first two columns of TableIII, Panel A, indicate an inverse relation between prior tax burdens and subsequent before-tax performance. A one-standard-deviation increase in the tax burden over the prior three years (1.18 percentage points) significantly decreases the before-tax return between 0.39 and 0.55 percentage points per year depending on whether we control for other fund characteristics. Thus, funds that tend to exhibit prior low tax burdens do not underperform on a pre-tax basis funds with higher tax burdens as one would expect if the higher tax efficiency substantially constrained asset management.
The two middle columns focus on the persistence of the tax burden. Ex ante, it is not clear whether tax burdens are persistent. On the one hand, the mutual funds' investment styles and strategies are persistent, resulting in persistent distributions and tax burdens. On the other hand, funds that made large taxable distributions in prior years will have a lower unrealized capital gains overhang, which might reduce current and future fund distributions. Our results indicate that the tax burden is highly persistent: a one-standard-deviation increase in the prior tax burden significantly increases next year's tax burden by between 0.44 and 0.47 percentage points per year.
The last two columns combine the predictive effects of the tax burden on future before-tax returns and tax burdens. A one-standard-deviation increase in the average tax burden over the past three years significantly decreases the after-tax return by between 0.87 and 0.99 percentage points per year. The predictability of after-tax returns by the prior tax burden is both statistically and economically significant. Around half of the after-tax performance effect is due to pre-tax performance and the other half is due to the persistence of the tax burden. Thus, taxable investors should take prior tax burdens into account when selecting mutual funds.
The remaining coefficients are broadly consistent with the mutual fund literature. Mutual fund pre-tax performance is persistent, as previously shown by Sharpe (1966), Grinblatt and Titman (1992), Hendricks, Patel, and Zeckhauser (1993), Brown and Goetzmann (1995), Carhart (1997), Bollen and Busse (2005), and Berk and van Binsbergen (2015), among many others. We also find that the pre-tax performance of funds decreases with the expense ratio (e.g., Gil-Bazo and Ruiz-Verdu (2009)), decreases with fund size (e.g., Chen et al. (2004), Pastor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2018)), and decreases with fund flows (e.g., Frazzini and Lamont (2009)).
The well-known difficulty of predicting mutual fund returns is apparent from the smallR2of the return regressions. Fund characteristics, time fixed effects, and style fixed effects can explain only around 3% of the variation in before-tax returns. In contrast, we find that around 12% of the variability in the tax burden can be explained by the factors considered here. This evidence suggests that taxable mutual fund investors should carefully consider the determinants of funds' future tax burdens, as it is more difficult to predict before-tax performance than tax burdens.
Panels B and C report performance results using style-year or fund, style, and year fixed effects. The results are qualitatively similar in these two specifications: the relation between the tax burden and the before-tax return is more pronounced using fund fixed and less pronounced using style-year fixed effects.
Our measure of the tax burden depends on the distributions of the funds and on the historical tax rates on the different types of distributions. Our results are qualitatively unaffected if we instead use weighted-average marginal tax rates following Sialm (2009) or the marginal tax rates from 2016, or if we incorporate capital gains taxes on fund investor liquidations, as summarized in TableIA.IIIin the Internet Appendix. The results also continue to hold if we exclude index funds (TableIA.IVin the Internet Appendix) or if we focus on mutual funds that hold more than 90% in domestic common stocks (TableIA.Vin the Internet Appendix).
While TableIIIstudies the association between the prior tax burden and fund returns, TableIVstudies the relation between the prior tax burden and various risk- and style-adjusted performance measures. We report the results using the CAPM, the three-factor model of Fama and French (1993), the four-factor model of Carhart (1997), the Fama and French (2015) five-factor model, the Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2015) four-factor model, the manipulation-proof performance measure of Goetzmann et al. (2007) using a risk-aversion coefficient of three, and the Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) measures of excess performance (i.e., the return of a fund before fees minus the return of the composite index fund benchmark) and value added (i.e., the excess performance multiplied by lagged asset size).18
We report adjusted performance measures using two different methodologies. The first estimates the factor loadings contemporaneously each year using daily observations. Fund performance,PERF, for the factor-based measures is computed as the annualized intercept of time series regressions using daily return data estimated separately each calendar year. For example, the abnormal return of fundfin yeartfor the CAPM (αf,tCAPM) is the intercept from the regressionRf,t−RTB,t=αf,tCAPM+βf,tCAPM(RM,t−RTB,t)+εf,tCAPM, whereRf,tdenotes the return of fundfon dayt,RM,tdenotes the market portfolio return, andRTB,tdenotes the Treasury bill rate. The difference between the before- and the after-tax alphas can be interpreted as a factor-adjusted tax burden and is summarized in the two middle columns. Since daily returns are available in CRSP only since 1999, we focus this analysis on the subsample between 1999 and 2016.
The second methodology estimates the factor loadings over a rolling 36-month window using monthly observations. Fund performance,PERF, here is defined as the fund return minus the fitted return for the various models. For example, the abnormal return for the CAPM is defined asPERFf,t=Rf,t−RTB,t−βf,t−1M(RM,t−RTB,t), whereRf,t,RM,t, andRTB,tmeasure the returns of the fund, the market, and Treasury bills in montht. The factor loadingβM,t−1of a fund is estimated on a rolling basis using monthly observations over the prior 36 months. The abnormal returns are averaged over a calendar year to obtain annual performance measures. Adjusted returns using the second methodology can be estimated for the full sample period from 1990 to 2016.
The rolling-window approach is problematic for several reasons. First, mutual funds change their factor exposures over time, as documented in prior literature (e.g., Cooper, Gulen, and Rau (2005), Brown, Harlow, and Zhang (2009), Wermers (2012)). Thus, the factor exposures estimated over the prior 36 months may not be appropriate in the current period. Second, the method using monthly data estimates factor loadings using only 36 monthly observations. It is difficult to precisely estimate factor exposures using few observations. The method using daily data estimates factor exposures using around 250 daily observations. Third, using 36-month rolling regressions is problematic because abnormal returns in adjacent years are based on overlapping data. The daily methodology estimates abnormal returns separately for each calendar year and does not rely on overlapping observations. However, the rolling-window approach with monthly data can be estimated over the full sample period from 1990 to 2016 and may be less affected by stale prices.
Panel A of TableIVreports the results using daily data. The coefficients on the prior tax burden remain economically and statistically significant after adjusting for common factors.19The association between the prior tax burden and the before-tax alpha weakens the most for the Fama-French five-factor model. Thus, low-tax-burden funds generate superior pre-tax returns because they load favorably on common factors.
Panel B reports the results using monthly data. The relation between the prior tax burden and before-tax performance is significantly negative after controlling for fund characteristics for all performance measures except the measures based on Berk and van Binsbergen. Their performance measures may be more noisy if estimated using monthly data.
In sum, our results show that the prior tax burden remains an important predictor for both before- and after-tax alpha. The results further indicate that flows by fund investors are not sufficient to equalize before- and after-tax returns across different funds.
The relation between the prior tax burden and subsequent performance is not necessarily an intentional consequence of fund management. Indeed, it is implausible that imposing tax constraints on portfolio optimization enhances investment opportunities. The positive relation between tax efficiency and pre-tax performance could be due to the fact that tax-efficient funds incur lower trading costs, benefit from favorable factor exposures, or select more highly skilled fund managers. In this section, we discuss possible explanations for the positive relation between tax efficiency and pre-tax performance.
The gross holdings return,RH, is defined as the value-weighted return of the previously disclosed fund holdings, where the weights correspond to the relative value of each fund position at the end of the previous month. The return gap,RG, captures the impact of unobserved actions on fund returns. It consists of the trading costsTCand the interim trading benefitsITB. Funds for which the trading costs exceed the interim trading benefits will exhibit negative return gaps.
Following Daniel et al. (1997), we further divide the gross holdings return into a characteristic selectivity measure,CS, a characteristic timing measure,CT, and an average style measure,AS. To form the benchmark portfolios, we group the universe of U.S. common stocks into quintiles along the dimensions of size (market value of equity), dividend yield (sum of dividends paid over the prior year divided by the prior stock price), and momentum (the return of a stock in the previous year excluding the most recent month). This sequential sorting results in 125 passive portfolios. This classification differs from Daniel et al. (1997) by replacing the industry-adjusted book-to-market ratio with the dividend yield. Using the dividend yield allows us to better capture stock characteristics that are directly related to securities' tax consequences.20
The variableCSis a measure of stock selection ability and uses as a benchmark the return of a portfolio of stocks that is matched to each of the fund's stock holdings each quarter along the dimensions of size, dividend yield, and momentum. The variableCTis a measure of style timing ability, which examines whether fund managers can generate additional performance by exploiting time-varying expected returns of the benchmark portfolios. Finally, theASmeasure captures the returns due to a fund's tendency to hold stocks with certain style characteristics.
TableVreports results from the performance regressions using the various performance components as dependent variables. We find that the prior tax burden is negatively related to the ability to select securities and to the average style, but style timing does not contribute to the performance difference between tax-efficient and tax-inefficient funds.
We also find a significant relation between the prior tax burden and trading costs. Low tax burden funds generate significantly lower trading costs, which indicates that these funds are capable of overall cost-efficient trading. In contrast, expenses are not significantly related to the tax burden after we control for lagged expenses and other fund characteristics. The interim trading benefit, which is computed as the difference between the return gap and estimated trading costs, is positively related to the prior tax burden. Thus, the positive relation between the tax burden and fund performance cannot be explained by unobserved actions captured by the interim trading benefit. Instead, it is influenced by longer term investment ability as captured by the holdings-based performance measures of Daniel et al. (1997).
Overall, the superior performance of tax-efficient funds can be explained primarily by their lower trading costs, their favorable style exposures, and their superior selectivity. These performance results provide evidence of selection effects: tax-efficient fund managers tend to exhibit superior investment ability along many different dimensions. We do not find support for the view that the constraints imposed by tax efficiency prevent mutual funds from taking advantage of their stock selection and style timing abilities.
The tax burden of mutual funds depends on the style of their investments, as discussed by Bergstresser and Pontiff (2013) and Israel and Moskowitz (2011). First, dividend yields differ across mutual fund styles. Large-capitalization stocks and value stocks tend to pay higher dividend yields than small-capitalization stocks and growth stocks. Second, the capital gains distributions also depend on investment styles. Funds that focus on small capitalization stocks or value stocks tend to sell stocks that recently appreciated in value to maintain their style exposures. Thus, small-capitalization and value funds might realize more capital gains.
To examine differences in tax burdens across styles, in TableVIwe report before- and after-tax returns for different style portfolios. The size score of a fund corresponds to the average quintile number of firms' market values using NYSE cutoff levels. The value and momentum scores are defined correspondingly based on the average quintile number of the book-to-market ratio and the prior-year return of a mutual fund's holdings.
The results indicate that mutual funds holding small-capitalization stocks and value stocks tend to outperform before taxes. This outperformance is driven primarily by the superior performance of the corresponding styles over our sample period and remains after adjusting the returns for taxes. In contrast, mutual funds with high momentum scores do not outperform other funds.
The results also show that the tax burden effect remains after controlling for funds' style scores: the prior tax burden is negatively related to future before- and after-tax returns and positively related to the future tax burden. The interaction effects between the tax burden and the style scores are generally insignificant for before-tax returns, indicating that the tax burden is not differentially related to pre-tax fund performance across different styles.22
Overall, we find that the prior tax burden is an important determinant of mutual fund selection even after conditioning on different investment styles. Funds that exhibit higher tax burdens over the prior three years will continue to exhibit high tax burdens.
While TableIIIreports results on performance predictability using the tax burden over the prior three years, TableVIIpresents results using the tax burden over the prior one, five, or 10 years. Using longer horizons helps smooth out cyclical variation in fund distributions, but reduces the sample size and requires overlapping observations for the prior tax burden. The results over these alternative horizons are consistent with the base-case results in TableIII.
Figure4shows that marginal tax rates on fund distributions have differed substantially over time. To examine whether the results vary across time, we divide our sample into the subperiods before and after the Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003. Both subperiods have approximately equal length (1990 to 2002, 2003 to 2016). We find that taxes and equity returns were higher during the first subperiod. Despite these differences, however, we find consistent results across the two subsamples, as summarized in TableVIII.23
The tax burden depends on the distributions of dividends and short- and long-term capital gains. TableIXdecomposes the tax burden into the different types of distributions to examine whether the results are driven by capital gain or dividend distributions. The distributions are measured over the prior three years.
Funds with lower prior long-term capital gains distributions exhibit higher returns before and after taxes. Similarly, we find a negative relation between prior dividend distributions and before- and after-tax returns. In contrast, we do not obtain a significant relation between short-term capital gain distributions and subsequent before-tax returns. As summarized in TableI, long-term capital gains account for most of the fund distributions and dividend and short-term capital gains distributions are relatively small. The middle two columns of TableIXshow that the future tax burden is positively related to all three types of distributions.
Taken together, these results indicate that the predictability of the tax burden does not come entirely from the type of stocks that funds hold. Rather, it comes mainly from the conscious and consistent trading decisions of fund managers regarding their capital gains realizations.
Mutual fund holdings in DC pension plans are an important segment of financial markets. For example, mutual fund holdings in tax-qualified accounts constituted more than half of mutual fund assets in 2015 according to the Investment Company Institute (ICI).24Although taxable fund investors care about the tax burdens of mutual funds, fund investors in tax-qualified accounts are not affected by the taxable distributions of their investments. Sialm and Starks (2012) find that funds held primarily by taxable investors choose investment strategies that result in lower tax burdens than funds held primarily in tax-qualified accounts.25
To investigate whether our results differ across mutual funds with different tax clienteles, in TableXwe control for the DC ratio and the interaction between the DC ratio and the prior tax burden. The number of observations decreases since the DC ratio is available only for the 1997 to 2016 subperiod and for a subset of funds. Consistent with our base-case specification, we find that tax-efficient funds without DC assets exhibit superior subsequent performance before taxes. Consistent with Sialm and Starks (2012), we find that the DC ratio is positively related to the tax burden and not related to future returns before taxes. The relation between the tax burden and pre-tax returns is most negative for funds with low DC ratios, as captured by the positive interaction effect. The negative relation between the tax burden and pre-tax returns in the multivariate specification holds for funds with DC ratios below 50%, which account for more than 85% of observations. Tax considerations naturally play a less important role for funds with high DC ratios. Thus, the pre-tax performance of tax-inefficient funds is particularly poor if the funds are held mostly by taxable investors.
article.V. Self-Designated Tax-Managed Funds:
In this section, we study the performance of mutual funds that explicitly classify themselves as tax-managed funds. Funds that include “tax-aware,” “tax-efficient,” “tax-managed,” or similar terms are classified as self-designated tax-managed funds.
Some mutual funds may be tax-efficient not because they deliberately minimize the tax burden, but because they use investment strategies that generate relatively low tax burdens as a byproduct. To address this possibility, we compare self-designated tax-managed funds with other not-tax-managed funds that follow similar investment strategies and are managed by the same fund families. Overall, our sample includes 79 funds that are self-designated tax-managed funds. Thus, the number of self-designated tax-managed funds accounts for a very small segment of domestic equity mutual funds.
TableXIreports the average annual excess returns of tax-managed funds and matched funds that do not explicitly manage their taxes. Standard errors are clustered by fund.
Panel A matches each self-designated tax-efficient fund to another fund in the same family, where the name differs only by omitting the designation as a tax-efficient fund. For example, the “J.P. Morgan Tax Aware Large Cap Growth Fund” is matched to the “J.P. Morgan Large Cap Growth Fund.” Fund families set up such twin funds to cater to different tax clienteles. We find twin funds only for 28 tax-efficient funds. The tax-efficient funds outperform the matched funds by 0.33 percentage points per year before taxes. However, the before-tax return differences are not statistically significant due to the very small number of tax-managed funds with valid matches. Consistent with our base-case results in TableIII, we find that the tax burdens and after-tax returns are significantly different across the two types of funds: the tax burden of tax-efficient funds is 1.61 percentage points lower than the burden of matched funds, and tax-efficient funds outperform matched funds by 1.95 percentage points per year after tax.
Panel B broadens the sample of valid fund pairs by matching a self-designated tax-efficient fund to the fund in the same family that has the closest style based on fund holdings. For each mutual fund, we compute the size, value, and momentum scores as described in SectionII.C. We find matches for 73 tax-efficient funds. For each potential pair of funds, we compute the average difference in the three style scores. The matched fund corresponds to the fund with the smallest average squared style score difference. We find that the tax-efficient funds outperform the matched funds only marginally before taxes. This performance difference is not close to being statistically significant. Consistent with Panel A, we find statistically and economically significant differences in the tax burdens and after-tax returns. The tax burden of tax-efficient funds is 0.78 percentage points lower than the burden of matched funds, and tax-efficient funds outperform matched funds by 0.81 percentage points per year after tax.
Panel C also incorporates fund size as a matching criterion. We obtain the closest match by minimizing the average squared difference between the standardized logarithm of the TNA and the three standardized style scores. In this sample, we have 70 pairs of matched funds. The results are similar to those in Panel B.
Overall, we find that self-designated tax-efficient funds significantly outperform after taxes similar funds without an explicit tax objective. Furthermore, the self-designated tax-efficient funds do not underperform other funds before taxes, indicating that the constraints imposed by tax-efficient asset management do not lead to significant performance consequences.26
The insignificant pre-tax performance difference indicates that the performance differences in our base-case analysis are due to investment styles or fund family effects. Thus, the results based on the matched analysis are consistent with our main results, which show that the superior performance of tax-efficient funds can be explained by trading costs, style exposures, and investment ability. These differences are largely eliminated when we compare tax-managed funds with other funds in the same families with similar names or styles.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Many mutual funds attempt to create value for their investors through active trading strategies. Such strategies lead to substantial trading costs and make it difficult for actively managed mutual funds to generate persistent superior performance for their investors, as discussed by French (2008). However, an additional cost of active fund management—one that is often ignored both in academia and in practice—is the tax imposed on fund investors. Although it is difficult for fund managers to generate persistent superior investment performance by relying purely on picking stocks or timing the market, it is relatively easy to avoid destroying value for taxable fund investors by managing investment taxes.
Our paper shows that investment taxes are of similar importance as fund expenses. Surprisingly, we find that funds that impose higher tax burdens on their investors do not offset these tax costs with superior before-tax performance. Rather, tax-efficient funds seem to outperform tax-inefficient funds both before and after taxes through superior investment ability, lower trading costs, and careful tax management. These results indicate that the flows by fund investors are not sufficiently sensitive to funds' prior tax burden to equalize fund returns before or after taxes.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
